author: @alexmacdiver
text:
Dear Sir
"Could you Fix it for me".  🏅
During your current winter project of Heating the Nation by Learn'n em  (perhaps while inadvertently burning council estate's to ashes for the entertainment of Biden & Rasputin iii)
Have you looked into the "Turkish Tea Maker Samovar / Semaver". ?
It encapsulates your departments agenda, buy heating water for a Brew, while burning/heating ones abode.
End of Message.....
"Let them eat Cake"  🍰

author: @georgeabraham7256
text: What could you do mixing polystyrene and graphene? How efficient can a static wind mill be.

author: @leesorenson6119
text: Are you cataloging your shows? I'm looking for the few about making the conductive ink that you used to make that heating panels. I'm wondering if you can package your videos of education or research. I'm really not a marketing guy but I agree with you gaining for your work. I'm on the UofM Flint campus and I talk you up to folks in town, but I am just a retired carpenter marveling at how far we've come. And pressing towards the mark.

author: @hamidijafri
text: thanks Robert for confirming the effect of tannin on graphite exfoliation...interesting.

author: @louisanderson6224
text: I have a hearing problem with "British" presenters,  I've visited Britain At least a dozen times, had no problem.  I think its a problem with my hearing and the accent.  The video's are great otherwise.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: cheers mate - not a lot I can do about my accent I am afraid - but I am glad you like the videos

author: @arainboldt
text:
Have you read about the recent developments in using Tea Tree to make graphene? 
http://phys.org/news/2015-08-scientists-high-quality-graphene-tea-tree.html

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: +bob very cool mate - thanks for the heads up

replies.author: @TonyGrant.
replies.text: That is very interesting

author: @thagrit
text: This is for all you who are looking to make your own graphene the tannic acid way. There is a source for the tannic acid that is cheaper than looking on ebay for gallotannic acid. It's called quebracho powder. I  was talking to my friend about this method. He told me about quebracho.  He had a partial container of it from a boiler chemical recipe. He no longer uses quebracho in his process but says it is cheap to buy large quantities. If you can't get it yourselves I will see if I can get more and provide it at my cost plus shipping.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: +thagrit brilliant share mate - cheers

replies.author: @thagrit
replies.text: +Robert Murray-Smith Thanks!

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: +thagrit no worries

author: @kalabaris
text: There is this company called Carbon3D that makes 3D objects using a projected uv ray to harden shapes from a pool of resin. I was wondering with your incite, if you added graphene to a pool of resin and used the same process, could the graphene harden to a strong object?

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: yeah no worries - the problem is getting it in there

author: @NINKNOONAN
text: I experimented with Green tea ranging from 2 tea bags per liter to 10 per liter (soaked for 1 hour cold deionized and sonicate for 12 hours and let stand for 2 weeks ). The challenge  I found I was growing a range of cultures on top of the solution. The result were actually very good once you poor off the mold and centrifuge  :-)  I think I am going to give up with that approach and try using just Gallotannic acid (but not as readily available as my wifes cupboard)

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: lol - I understand and went for gallotanic for those reasons too

author: @ravemaster2000
text: ive got an idea, use a dremmel as a lab homogenizer and... what if u run the graphene through a pressure washer into a metal bucket, and cycle it through for a couple of hours? I wonder if one of us could develop an erosion method where the graphite can be eroded by a waterfalling method blasting the graphite through a pressure washer over course steel with a pressure washer recycling the water/graphene mixture......... this could work very well! please try it? :)

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: it's a good idea - why don't you try it? it could be huge lol

replies.author: @ravemaster2000
replies.text: it could be the magic method

replies.author: @ravemaster2000
replies.text: i will try it as soon as i have a way!

replies.author: @ravemaster2000
replies.text: this method carves canyons why not graphine? haha

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text:
Christopher Rockman
yep good point lol

author: @podgypig120
text: Hi Rob. What is the graphite company you mention here? Can't quite make out the name.. graph excel?

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: yep - but they don't sell to the public mate - you have to be ready to buy 100kgs or so

author: @Venturestarx
text: Who was the jerk who didn't give you credit? That person needs a flogging.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: I am tempted to tell you - but he knows who he is and I hope by mentioning this he will be a little ashamed - But, I get this kind of thing all the time - particularly from students - They are always asking me to write their dissertations if you can believe it - and we are not talking about college students here - I have had five doctoral thesis requests this year alone - 3 genuine and 2 who want me to do the work so they can publish - incredible!! - it is so often that when you do open source some people inevitably think you are a fool and there to be taken advantage of.

replies.author: @Venturestarx
replies.text: Robert Murray-Smith Ugh, my wife and I think that is disgusting (she is a grad student) but if you do want to publish anything, I'll help. And it will curb the paper stealing. You get enough out there that it would be hilarious. :)

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text:
Venturestarx
cheers mate - i was mulling over starting a journal actually - for the league

replies.author: @Venturestarx
replies.text: Rad.

author: @ArkiverUnifiedEnergy
text:
Glad to share. Tea- the answer's right under our nose. 
Cheers

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: you have a lot of good ideas mate

author: @actudoran
text:
hi pal!
awesome vid! tea ... really ... :) wow 
although ... have you noticed, if you take it easy on the milk in your cuppa, when getting cold you can make out the thin film on top of the tea (limescale I thought ... ) pretty similar to single layer structures ... I can get the similarity of the two :) 
ah well ... the benefits of the well deserved cuppa when you need one ... makes one wonder ... :))

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: lol - depends on your water area mate - I used distilled water - no film! - though being from yorkshire I drink the stuff by the gallon lol - by the way I have a mild obsession with tea - wait til the next vid

replies.author: @actudoran
replies.text: haha can't wait :)

text: I love your work and I hope you get the credit you deserve soon enough.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering

author: @MaleAdaptor
text: Robert you seem quite generous in nature. I wish I too could laugh off others taking credit for my work. You not being acknowledged made my blood boil!

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: it's just the way it goes mate - it does annoy me as I put this stuff up there for everyone and i have just found a few papers out there where I know the people who have written them as we were discussing the ideas and I gave them a bit of help just to find they then claimed it as their own - it is a bit sad as sooner or later they will be found out - ah well lol
